disclaimer: This file has NO measured or benchmarked latency numbers anywhere in this codebase or its docs. Every illustrativeLatencyMs range below is a rough, invented placeholder for the load simulator's UI only (empirical: false); do not present it as measured. The two poll-interval constants under toolRelayPolling are the only real numbers here (read directly from services/runner/src/tools/relay.ts), marked empirical: true.

structuralFacts.fact: Every /run starts a fresh sandbox-agent daemon and ACP harness process tree and tears it down in the finally path once the turn ends. Nothing warm is kept between turns.

The runtime is cold: the client resends full message history on every turn. The InMemorySessionPersistDriver the sandbox-agent engine keeps exists only for the duration of one /run process and never survives across turns, so there is no warm daemon or persisted model session to reuse.

On the Daytona sandbox, the runner creates a remote VM and pushes the harness login, Pi extension, AGENTS.md, skills, and system-prompt files over the Daytona filesystem API before the harness can even start. Agenta ships a build recipe, not a pre-built snapshot, unless the operator builds one; a pre-baked snapshot avoids a slow per-run pi CLI install but VM creation itself is still a cold remote-provisioning step on every turn.

The runner sidecar is one long-lived Node HTTP server on :8765 with no worker pool. Each concurrent /run request is handled by the Node event loop and spawns its own process tree (daemon + ACP adapter + harness CLI); there is no per-request process reuse or queueing layer in front of it beyond what Node's async I/O provides.

A client disconnect (streaming) or the end of a batch call tears the sandbox down in a finally block. An AbortSignal is wired into HTTP streaming specifically so a dropped browser connection still tears down the sandbox rather than leaking it.

Daytona sandboxes are created ephemeral (auto-delete on stop) paired with a non-zero SANDBOX_AGENT_DAYTONA_AUTOSTOP_MINUTES (default 15). If the runner process is killed and skips its own per-run teardown, the sandbox self-reaps after the idle window instead of burning credit indefinitely.

The tool relay poll loop grows its own delay under idle load: it starts at RELAY_POLL_MS and, after RELAY_POLL_IDLE_GROW_AFTER consecutive idle polls with no new request file, doubles geometrically up to RELAY_POLL_MAX_MS. Any new request file resets the delay immediately. This matters most on Daytona, where each poll is a remote `ls` exec, and for client-tool-only turns that wait on a browser response and produce no other tool traffic.

Today's runner enforces no concurrency limit and no queue anywhere: every POST /run (or /stream) starts executing immediately. The only bookkeeping is an in-memory Set of in-flight sandbox handles, kept solely so a shutdown signal can best-effort tear them down; it never gates or delays a new run. Under overload, local-sandbox runs degrade through CPU/RAM contention in the shared container rather than waiting in line; Daytona runs each get their own VM and fan out until the provider's own account quota is hit. The load simulator's 'runner concurrency' slider models a what-if bounded pool, not this system's actual behavior.

stages.notes: HTTP request/response over the public edge; batch or SSE-framed stream.
In-process dispatch and AgentConfig.from_params coercion inside the shared services container.

Zero or more sequential HTTP round trips to agenta-api (POST /tools/resolve per gateway tool, POST /secrets/resolve for named secrets). Scales with the number of declared gateway/code tools; builtin and client tools add nothing here.
HTTP to the sidecar's single-process server, or a CLI subprocess spawn when AGENTA_RUNNER_INTERNAL_URL is unset (source checkout). No connection pool concerns beyond ordinary keep-alive HTTP.

Structural fact: runner-single-http-server. Node's event loop interleaves concurrent /run requests; there is no worker-pool boundary, so contention shows up as event-loop scheduling delay under many concurrent runs, not queueing at an explicit gate.

Structural fact: cold-sandbox-per-turn. Spawn the sandbox-agent daemon, the ACP adapter (pi-acp or claude-agent-acp), and the harness CLI, all on the runner host. Claude's first run per host also downloads the claude CLI over HTTPS.

Structural fact: daytona-vm-start-dominates. Remote VM creation plus pushing login/extension/context files over the Daytona filesystem API. A pre-baked snapshot with pi installed avoids the per-run pi CLI install step but not VM creation itself.
The model-call / tool-call loop inside the harness. Dominated by LLM latency and however many tool round trips the model chooses to make; not a fixed runner-side cost.
Structural fact: tool-relay-idle-backoff. Governs how quickly a relayed tool call (Daytona, or the non-Pi internal MCP channel even on local) is picked up by the runner.
One POST /tools/call round trip (direct, or via the file relay) plus the Composio provider call itself.

Not a system-latency stage at all: an ask verdict pauses the turn for a human, which can be seconds or hours. Structural fact: the pause is a real turn boundary (stopReason: "paused"), not a retry loop.
Structural fact: teardown-on-finally. Process tree shutdown (local) or sandbox stop (Daytona, backstopped by daytona-leak-backstop if the runner is killed).
Pi: read the AGENTA_USAGE_OUT writeback file. Both harnesses: stamp usage on the live workflow span. Spans themselves export asynchronously to the OTel pipeline and do not block the response.
Batch: one JSON response. Streaming: the SSE stream has already been delivering parts live; this is just the final [DONE].
